messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Stable Isotope Mixing Models using SIBER, SIAR, MixSIAR (SIMM09)
messages.subject_clean: Stable Isotope Mixing Models using SIBER, SIAR, MixSIAR (SIMM09)
messages.body_plain:
Stable Isotope Mixing Models using SIBER, SIAR, MixSIAR (SIMM09)

https://www.prstatistics.com/course/stable-isotope-mixing-models-using-r-simm09/

25th - 28th April 2023

This course will cover the concepts, technical background and use of stable
isotope mixing models (SIMMs) with a particular focus on running them in R.
This course will cover the concepts, technical background and use of stable
isotope mixing models (SIMMs) with a particular focus on running them in R.
Recently SIMMs have become a very popular tool for quantifying food webs
and thus the diet of predators and prey in an ecosystem. Starting with only
basic understanding of statistical models, we will cover the do?s and
don?ts of using SIMMs with a particular focus on the widely used package
SIAR and the more advanced MixSIAR. Participants will be taught some of the
advanced features of these packages, which will enable them to produce a
richer class of output, and are encouraged to bring their own data sets and
problems to study during the round-table discussions.

Please email oliverhooker at prstatistics.com with any questions

Upcoming courses can be found here
<https://www.prstatistics.com/live-courses/>

-- 

Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR statistics

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] ONLINE COURSE – Species Distribution Modeling using R (SDMR05) This course will be delivered live
messages.subject_clean: ONLINE COURSE – Species Distribution Modeling using R (SDMR05) This course will be delivered live
messages.body_plain:
ONLINE COURSE ? Species Distribution Modeling using R (SDMR05) This course
will be delivered live

https://www.prstatistics.com/course/species-distribution-modeling-using-r-sdmr05/

Start date - Tuesday, April 25th, 2023

*ABOUT THIS COURSE* - If you are interested in gaining the introductory
knowledge required to work with SDMs, whether you be a student, postdoc, or
practicing scientist, this course is for you. This 8 half day course will
provide participants with the background knowledge and skills needed to get
started in the use of species distribution models (SDMs) for applied and
basic research. The course will focus on (1) the preparation of required
spatial datasets (biological observations and environmental predictors);
(2) practical considerations in the development, application, and
interpretation of SDMs; and (3) fitting and evaluating SDMs using different
statistical approaches ? all using R.

Using a combination of lectures, coding exercises in R, and case studies,
participants will learn to:

   - Understand background theory and model assumptions
   - Identify, manipulate and prepare spatial datasets for SDMs
   - Fit, interpret, and evaluate SDMs using several statistical methods
   (e.g., Maxent, Mahalanobis distance, generalized linear models, boosted
   regression trees)
   - Project SDMs to predict climate change impacts, etc.

The course is entirely R-based and while techniques of working with spatial
data in R will be covered in detail, prior experience with R is highly
recommended. If you are new to R, this course will be of most use to you if
you work through a few tutorials to understand the basics of R programming
before the start of the course. Students are highly encouraged to bring
their own data sets, but this is not required for participation.

Course material will be presented by Matt Fitzpatrick who has published
broadly in the use of SDMs for applied and basic science.

Please email oliverhooker at prstatistics.com with any questions

Upcoming courses can be found here
<https://www.prstatistics.com/live-courses/>

-- 

Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR statistics

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Course: Time series analysis and forecasting in R
messages.subject_clean: Course: Time series analysis and forecasting in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
registration is now open for the course time series analysis and forecasting in R!
 
Dates: online, 4-8 September
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/time-series-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/time-series-in-r/ )
 
 
In this course you will learn how to wrangle, visualize and explore ecological time series. You will also learn to use the {mvgam} and {brms} packages to analyse a diversity of ecological time series to gain useful insights and produce accurate forecasts. All course materials (presentations, practical exercises, data files, and commented R scripts) will be provided electronically to participants. 
 
 

By the end of the course, participants will be able to:
 
1.    Understand how dynamic GLMs and GAMs work to capture both nonlinear covariate effects and temporal dependence
2.    Be able to fit dynamic GLMs and GAMs in R using the {mvgam} and {brms} packages
3.    Understand how to critique, visualize and compare fitted dynamic models
4.    Know how to produce forecasts from dynamic models and evaluate their accuracies using probabilistic scoring rules
 
 
Best regards,
Carlo
 
 
 
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

mobile: +49 17645230846

Follow us on [ Twitter ]( https://twitter.com/Physacourses ) & [ Mastodon ]( https://mas.to/@PhysaliaCourses )

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] course Statistics in R for biologists and ecologists
messages.subject_clean: course Statistics in R for biologists and ecologists
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
registration is now open for the 2nd edition of the course Statistics in R for biologists and ecologists.
 
Dates: online, July 17th-20th
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course13/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course13/ )
 
This course will introduce scientists and practitioners interested in applying statistical approaches in their daily routine using R as a working environment. Participants will be introduced into the mysteries of R and R Studio while learning how to perform common statistical analyses. After a short introduction on R and its principles, the focus will be on questions that could be addressed using common statistical analyses, both for descriptive statistics and for statistical inference.
Full list of our courses and Workshops: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/ )
 
 
Should you have any questions, please feel free to contact us: info at physalia-courses.org 

Best regards,
Carlo
 
 
 
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Course - Remote Sensing in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
I hope this email finds you well. We are excited to announce our upcoming course, Remote Sensing in R, which is tailored to scholars who want to learn about ecological remote sensing problems and its solutions using R.
Our course is designed for beginners, and we will start from scratch to ensure that everyone understands the concepts and can code remotely sensed analysis with ease. The course will be conducted remotely, which means you can participate from anywhere in the world and at any time.
By the end of the course, you will have the skills to code your analysis workflows and report the output in LaTeX. We have included a dedicated section at the end of the course on the basics of LaTeX scripting, which covers both articles and presentations.
For more information, please check it out: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/remote-sensing-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/remote-sensing-in-r/ )
 
Best regards,
Carlo
 
 
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org
